narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, including your plan to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 93% chance that you would fail it, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high predicted failure risk means the school views you as a student who, based on patterns from past applicants, is unlikely to succeed on that critical exam right away. The decision hinged most heavily on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school, particularly your Year 3 performance, which placed you in the 2nd decile—well below the average 5th or 6th decile for admitted students who typically pass the bar. This low ranking was the strongest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure, as it signals to the model a trajectory of underperformance compared to peers who excel in those metrics. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also far below the average around 5.7, reinforced this concern as the third-most influential factor, further tilting the prediction against you. Even your LSAT score of 28, significantly lower than the 36.7 average, ranked second in influence and strongly contributed to the failure prediction by highlighting a gap in standardized test readiness. Your middle family income bracket didn't sway things much either way, but these core academic signals dominated the model's thinking. In essence, while your college grades were a bright spot, your school deciles and LSAT painted a picture riskier than typical successful bar takers, who cluster higher in those areas. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your high school-like decile performance through additional coursework or test prep, and reapplying next cycle with updated credentials that align more closely with proven bar passers. Schools value persistence, and targeted improvements here could flip the model's outlook in your favor.
